title: Welcome to Arrays 📚
description: Store ordered lists of data in a single variable.

slides.title: The List 📝
slides.content:
So far, a variable has only held ONE piece of data (`age = 25` or `name = "Bob"`).

What if you wanted a variable to represent an entire grocery list of 50 items?

You need an **Array**.

slides.title: Creating an Array
slides.content:
An Array is an ordered list of items. 

You create an Array by wrapping your items in square brackets `[ ]` and separating them with commas.

```ruby
grocery_list = ["Milk", "Eggs", "Bread", "Apples"]
puts grocery_list
```

slides.title: Arrays in Action
slides.content: Run the code! Notice how Ruby naturally understands it is dealing with a list of things.

quizData.question: Which symbols are used to create an Array?
quizData.explanation: Square brackets `[ ]` represent an Array list in nearly all major programming languages.

slides.title: Accessing Items 🗃️
slides.content:
Arrays are strictly ordered. To grab a specific item out of the list, you stick square brackets directly onto the variable name and provide an Index Number.

`puts grocery_list[0]`

REMEMBER: Ruby is **0-indexed**. The very first item is ALWAYS at position `0`.

slides.title: Grabbing by Index
slides.content: Let's grab the second item in the list.
slides.codeSnippet:
colors = ["Red", "Green", "Blue"]

# Because we start at 0, index 1 is actually the second item!
puts colors[1]

quizData.question: In the array `letters = ["A", "B", "C"]`, what happens if you call `letters[0]`?
quizData.explanation: Counting begins at 0. So index `0` represents the first item universally.

slides.title: Negative Indexes 🔙
slides.content:
A beautiful Ruby trick: You can pass *negative* index numbers to count backward from the end of the array.

`letters[-1]` will always grab the completely last item in the list!
